content.body: A disease is any condition that impairs normal body function. Communicable (infectious) diseases are caused by pathogens (microorganisms) and can be transmitted between hosts.

Types of pathogens:
• Bacteria: living cells, treated by antibiotics
• Viruses: not cells, need host to replicate, treated by antivirals/vaccines
• Fungi: eukaryotes, treated by antifungals
• Protists (protozoa): eukaryotes (e.g. Plasmodium causing malaria)
• Prions: misfolded proteins (e.g. BSE)
• Parasitic worms: helminths (e.g. tapeworms)

content.title: Transmission Routes
content.body:
Direct contact: STIs, ringworm
Droplet/airborne: influenza, TB, COVID-19
Faecal-oral: cholera, typhoid (contaminated water/food)
Vector: malaria (Anopheles mosquito), sleeping sickness (tsetse fly)
Blood-borne: HIV, hepatitis B
Zoonotic: rabies (animal bite)

content.title: Malaria in Uganda
content.body:
Plasmodium parasite transmitted by female Anopheles mosquito
Symptoms: fever, chills, sweating (every 48-72 hours)
Life cycle: liver stage → blood stage → gametocytes
Prevention: mosquito nets, repellent, draining stagnant water, antimalarial drugs
Treatment: artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT)

questions.question: A pathogen is
questions.answer: a disease-causing microorganism
questions.explanation: A pathogen is specifically an organism (bacterium, virus, fungus, protist) that causes disease in its host.

questions.question: Antibiotics are effective against
questions.answer: bacteria
questions.explanation: Antibiotics target bacterial cell structures (cell wall, ribosomes). They do not work on viruses.

questions.question: Malaria is caused by
questions.answer: Plasmodium (a protist/protozoan)
questions.explanation: Malaria is caused by Plasmodium parasites (P. falciparum is most deadly) transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes.

questions.question: The vector for malaria is
questions.answer: female Anopheles mosquito
questions.explanation: Only female Anopheles mosquitoes transmit Plasmodium because they feed on blood (for egg development).

questions.question: Cholera is spread through
questions.answer: contaminated water and food (faecal-oral route)
questions.explanation: Vibrio cholerae bacteria are excreted in faeces. Drinking contaminated water causes infection — kills through severe dehydration.

questions.question: Which disease is airborne/droplet-transmitted?
questions.answer: Tuberculosis (TB)
questions.explanation: TB (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) spreads through coughing/sneezing droplets containing bacteria.

questions.question: Viruses differ from bacteria in that they
options: can only replicate inside host cells
questions.answer: can only replicate inside host cells
questions.explanation: Viruses are not cells — they have no metabolism and can only reproduce by injecting genetic material into host cells.

questions.question: Sleeping sickness in Africa is transmitted by
questions.answer: tsetse fly
questions.explanation: African sleeping sickness (caused by Trypanosoma) is transmitted by tsetse fly bites.

questions.question: The best prevention for malaria in Uganda is
options: using insecticide-treated bed nets and draining stagnant water
questions.answer: using insecticide-treated bed nets and draining stagnant water
questions.explanation: ITNs prevent mosquito bites at night (when Anopheles feeds). Eliminating stagnant water removes mosquito breeding sites.

questions.question: HIV is transmitted through
questions.answer: blood, sexual contact, and mother-to-child
questions.explanation: HIV is blood-borne and sexually transmitted. Mother-to-child transmission can occur during birth or breastfeeding.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: The immune system defends the body against pathogens using physical barriers, non-specific responses, and specific immune responses involving white blood cells.

Lines of defence:
1st line (physical barriers): skin, mucus, cilia, stomach acid, tears
2nd line (non-specific): phagocytosis, inflammation, fever, interferons
3rd line (specific immunity): lymphocytes — B cells and T cells

content.title: Non-specific Response
content.body:
Phagocytosis by neutrophils and macrophages:
1. Pathogen detected (opsonisation)
2. Phagocyte extends pseudopodia around pathogen
3. Pathogen engulfed in phagosome
4. Lysosomes fuse → enzymes digest pathogen
5. Debris released

Inflammation: increased blood flow, phagocytes recruited, may cause fever

Specific Immunity:
Humoral immunity (B cells):
Antigen detected → B cell activated → plasma cells → antibodies
Antibodies are specific to one antigen; cause agglutination, neutralisation
Memory B cells provide long-term immunity

Cell-mediated immunity (T cells):
CD4+ helper T cells: activate B cells and cytotoxic T cells
CD8+ cytotoxic T cells: kill infected cells directly
Regulatory T cells: suppress immune response

questions.question: The first line of defence against pathogens is
options: physical barriers like skin and mucus
questions.answer: physical barriers like skin and mucus
questions.explanation: Skin, mucus membranes, cilia, stomach acid, and tears form the first physical/chemical barrier against pathogens.

questions.question: Phagocytosis is carried out by
questions.answer: phagocytes (neutrophils and macrophages)
questions.explanation: Phagocytes engulf and digest pathogens by forming a phagosome and fusing it with lysosomes containing digestive enzymes.

questions.question: Antibodies are produced by
questions.answer: B cells (plasma cells)
questions.explanation: When activated by an antigen, B cells differentiate into plasma cells that produce specific antibodies.

questions.question: An antibody is specific because
options: it is produced by T cells
it only binds to one specific antigen (complementary shape)
questions.answer: it only binds to one specific antigen (complementary shape)
questions.explanation: Each antibody has a unique antigen-binding site (variable region) complementary to one specific antigen.

questions.question: Memory cells provide long-term immunity because
options: they produce antibodies immediately upon first exposure
they survive and respond faster upon re-exposure
they are produced in larger numbers
questions.answer: they survive and respond faster upon re-exposure
questions.explanation: Memory B and T cells persist after an infection. On re-exposure, they rapidly proliferate and respond before illness develops.

questions.question: CD4+ helper T cells are important because
options: they activate B cells and cytotoxic T cells
questions.answer: they activate B cells and cytotoxic T cells
questions.explanation: Helper T cells are coordinators of the immune response — they release cytokines that activate both humoral and cell-mediated immunity.

questions.question: HIV causes AIDS by
options: destroying CD4+ helper T cells, collapsing the immune response
questions.answer: destroying CD4+ helper T cells, collapsing the immune response
questions.explanation: HIV infects and destroys CD4+ T cells. Without them, the immune response fails, leaving the patient vulnerable to opportunistic infections.

questions.question: Vaccination works by
options: giving a weakened/killed pathogen or antigen to stimulate memory cell production without causing disease
questions.answer: giving a weakened/killed pathogen or antigen to stimulate memory cell production without causing disease
questions.explanation: Vaccines expose the immune system to antigens, stimulating B and T cell responses and memory cell formation without full disease.

questions.question: Inflammation after infection causes
options: redness, heat, swelling to increase blood flow and recruit immune cells
questions.answer: redness, heat, swelling to increase blood flow and recruit immune cells
questions.explanation: Inflammatory response: vasodilation increases blood flow (redness, heat), capillaries leak fluid (swelling), phagocytes recruited.

questions.question: Herd immunity is achieved when
options: enough of a population is immune that disease cannot spread effectively
questions.answer: enough of a population is immune that disease cannot spread effectively
questions.explanation: Herd immunity: when ~70-95% are immune, chains of transmission break, protecting even unvaccinated individuals.

questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA

content.body:
Non-specific defences (innate immunity):
Skin barrier, mucus, cilia, tears (lysozyme)
Phagocytosis: neutrophils/macrophages engulf pathogens
Inflammation, fever response

Specific defences (adaptive immunity):
T lymphocytes: cell-mediated immunity
• Helper T: activate B cells and cytotoxic T
• Cytotoxic T: kill infected cells
B lymphocytes: humoral immunity
• Produce specific antibodies
• Memory B cells (long-term immunity)

Antibody structure: Y-shaped glycoprotein
Antigen-antibody binding: complementary fit

content.title: Immune Response
content.body:
Primary response (first exposure):
• Lag phase (5-10 days)
• Antibody level rises then falls
• Memory cells formed

Secondary response (re-exposure):
• Faster and larger antibody production
• Antibody level much higher
• Effective before symptoms appear
• Basis of vaccination

content.title: Vaccines and Types
content.body:
Vaccines: introduce antigen without disease
Types: live attenuated (MMR), killed/inactivated (flu injection), subunit (hepatitis B), toxoid (tetanus), mRNA (COVID-19)
Herd immunity threshold: typically 80-95%
Adjuvants: added to boost immune response

questions.question: Non-specific immunity includes
questions.answer: phagocytosis, skin barrier, mucus
questions.explanation: Non-specific (innate): same response to all pathogens. Immediate, no memory

questions.question: B lymphocytes produce
questions.answer: antibodies
questions.explanation: B cells differentiate into plasma cells secreting specific antibodies against specific antigens

questions.question: Memory cells ensure that secondary immune response is
questions.answer: faster and stronger
questions.explanation: Memory B and T cells persist: on re-exposure respond rapidly producing high antibody levels before disease develops

questions.question: Antibodies are
questions.answer: Y-shaped glycoproteins binding specific antigens
questions.explanation: Immunoglobulins: Y-shape with variable region at tips complementary to specific antigen

questions.question: Vaccination works by
options: introducing antigen to stimulate immune response and memory cell formation without causing disease
questions.answer: introducing antigen to stimulate immune response and memory cell formation without causing disease
questions.explanation: Vaccines prime immune system: memory cells ready for rapid response if real pathogen encountered

questions.question: HIV destroys which cells?
questions.answer: Helper T cells (CD4+)
questions.explanation: HIV targets Helper T cells — coordinator of immune response. Depletes them → AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency)

questions.question: Passive immunity from antibodies (e.g. mother→baby via breast milk)
questions.answer: is short-lived (no memory cells formed)
questions.explanation: Passive: receive antibodies, no antigen → no memory cell formation → protection temporary

questions.question: Interferons are proteins that
options: signal nearby cells to produce antiviral proteins, slowing viral spread
questions.answer: signal nearby cells to produce antiviral proteins, slowing viral spread
questions.explanation: Interferons: released by virus-infected cells, alert neighbouring cells to produce antiviral defences

questions.question: Autoimmune disease occurs when
options: immune system attacks body's own tissues
immunity is too strong against pathogens
questions.answer: immune system attacks body's own tissues
questions.explanation: Autoimmunity: self-tolerance breakdown. Examples: Type 1 diabetes (attacks β cells), rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis

questions.question: Monoclonal antibodies used in medicine are
options: single-specificity antibodies produced from cloned B cells — extremely specific
questions.answer: single-specificity antibodies produced from cloned B cells — extremely specific
questions.explanation: Monoclonal antibodies: identical antibodies from one clone. Used in pregnancy tests, cancer therapy (Herceptin), COVID tests

content.body: Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, worms). They can spread between individuals and cause significant global health challenges.

content.title: Major Infectious Diseases
content.body:
Malaria: Plasmodium parasite, transmitted by Anopheles mosquito
Symptoms: fever, chills, anaemia. Kills ~600,000/year
Control: mosquito nets, insecticides, antimalarials

Tuberculosis (TB): Mycobacterium tuberculosis
Airborne: affects lungs. 1.5 million deaths/year
BCG vaccine. Drug-resistant TB (MDR-TB) crisis

HIV/AIDS: retrovirus, sexual/blood transmission
Antiretroviral therapy (ART) effective but no cure
Stigma remains a major health challenge

content.title: Cholera, Typhoid in Uganda
content.body:
Cholera: Vibrio cholerae (bacteria), faecal-oral route, contaminated water
Causes severe diarrhoea → dehydration → death
ORS (oral rehydration salts) treatment
Control: clean water, sanitation, hygiene

Typhoid: Salmonella typhi, contaminated food/water
Vaccine available
Both preventable with improved WASH (water, sanitation, hygiene)

questions.question: Malaria is caused by
options: Plasmodium protozoan transmitted by Anopheles mosquito
questions.answer: Plasmodium protozoan transmitted by Anopheles mosquito
questions.explanation: Malaria: Plasmodium falciparum (most dangerous), P. vivax. Female Anopheles transmits sporozoites

questions.question: Tuberculosis (TB) is transmitted by
questions.answer: airborne droplets (coughing/sneezing)
questions.explanation: TB: Mycobacterium in droplet nuclei. Inhaled, infects lungs. Crowding, malnutrition increase risk

questions.question: Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS) treat cholera by
options: replacing fluids and electrolytes lost through diarrhoea
questions.answer: replacing fluids and electrolytes lost through diarrhoea
questions.explanation: ORS: water + salts + sugar replaces the massive fluid loss from severe cholera diarrhoea

questions.question: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) for HIV
options: prevents HIV from replicating (suppresses viral load to undetectable)
questions.answer: prevents HIV from replicating (suppresses viral load to undetectable)
questions.explanation: ART: combinations of drugs block HIV reverse transcriptase and protease. Doesn't eliminate HIV but controls it

questions.question: BCG vaccine protects against
questions.answer: tuberculosis
questions.explanation: BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin): live attenuated M. bovis vaccine, protects especially against severe childhood TB

questions.question: Drug-resistant TB (MDR-TB) is a crisis because
options: standard antibiotics no longer effective — treatment is longer, more toxic, more expensive
questions.answer: standard antibiotics no longer effective — treatment is longer, more toxic, more expensive
questions.explanation: MDR-TB: resistant to isoniazid and rifampicin (first-line). XDR-TB resistant to most drugs. Major global health threat

questions.question: WASH stands for
questions.answer: Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene
questions.explanation: WASH: fundamental public health framework addressing access to clean water, safe sanitation, good hygiene practices

questions.question: Vector of malaria is
questions.answer: female Anopheles mosquito
questions.explanation: Vector: organism transmitting pathogen. Female Anopheles takes blood meal, injects Plasmodium sporozoites

questions.question: Epidemiology studies
options: distribution and determinants of disease in populations
questions.answer: distribution and determinants of disease in populations
questions.explanation: Epidemiology: who gets disease, where, when, why — informs public health interventions

questions.question: Herd immunity threshold for measles (~95%) means
options: when ≥95% immune, outbreaks cannot spread (each case infects <1 new person)
questions.answer: when ≥95% immune, outbreaks cannot spread (each case infects <1 new person)
questions.explanation: R₀ of measles ~15: each case infects 15 unvaccinated. Need very high coverage to break transmission chains

questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis

One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy

questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)

options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Vaccines and medicines have transformed global health, reducing death from infectious diseases. Understanding how they work and global health challenges is important for S4 Biology.

Vaccine types:
1. Live attenuated: weakened pathogen (MMR, oral polio, BCG)
2. Killed/inactivated: dead pathogen (flu shot, hepatitis A)
3. Subunit/recombinant: protein antigens only (HBV, HPV)
4. Toxoid: inactivated toxin (tetanus, diphtheria)
5. mRNA: genetic instructions for antigen (COVID-19 Pfizer/Moderna)
6. Viral vector: adenovirus carrying antigen gene (AstraZeneca COVID)

content.title: Antibiotic Resistance
content.body:
Antibiotics kill susceptible bacteria
Resistant bacteria survive and reproduce
Mutations: chance mutations give resistance genes
Natural selection: resistant bacteria outcompete susceptible
Prevention:
• Complete antibiotic courses
• Don't use for viral infections
• Don't share prescriptions
• Develop new antibiotics
• Limit agricultural use

content.title: Global Health Challenges
content.body:
Malaria: Plasmodium falciparum, mosquito vector, kills ~600,000/year
TB: Mycobacterium tuberculosis, airborne, 1.5M deaths/year
HIV/AIDS: retrovirus, 38M infected worldwide
Non-communicable: diabetes, cancer, cardiovascular (now >60% of deaths)
Uganda burden: malaria, HIV, TB, increasing NCDs

questions.question: Vaccines work by
options: introducing antigen to stimulate immune memory without causing disease
questions.answer: introducing antigen to stimulate immune memory without causing disease
questions.explanation: Vaccines: introduce harmless form of antigen → B cells produce antibodies + memory cells → rapid response if infected later

questions.question: mRNA vaccines (like COVID-19 Pfizer) work by
options: delivering mRNA instructing cells to make viral protein antigen, triggering immune response
questions.answer: delivering mRNA instructing cells to make viral protein antigen, triggering immune response
questions.explanation: mRNA vaccine: lipid nanoparticle delivers mRNA → ribosomes make spike protein → immune system recognises and responds

questions.question: Antibiotic resistance evolves because
options: resistant bacteria exist naturally and are selected when antibiotics kill susceptible ones
questions.answer: resistant bacteria exist naturally and are selected when antibiotics kill susceptible ones
questions.explanation: Natural selection: rare resistant mutants survive antibiotic treatment → reproduce → resistant strain takes over

questions.question: Taking antibiotics for viral infections is
questions.answer: useless and promotes resistance
questions.explanation: Antibiotics target bacteria — viruses lack bacterial cell walls/ribosomes. Taking antibiotics unnecessarily selects for resistance

questions.question: Primary vs secondary immune response: secondary is faster because
options: memory B and T cells rapidly produce antibodies
questions.answer: memory B and T cells rapidly produce antibodies
questions.explanation: Memory cells persist after first exposure; second encounter: rapid large antibody response — basis of vaccine protection

questions.question: Malaria is transmitted by
questions.answer: Anopheles mosquito (vector)
questions.explanation: Plasmodium parasites transmitted via bite of female Anopheles mosquito. Mosquito is vector — transmits but not reservoir

questions.question: BCG vaccine protects against
questions.answer: tuberculosis (TB)
questions.explanation: BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin): live attenuated Mycobacterium bovis. Standard vaccine against TB in Uganda and many countries

questions.question: Herd immunity threshold for measles (~95%) means
options: when 95% immune, virus cannot maintain transmission — even unvaccinated individuals protected
questions.answer: when 95% immune, virus cannot maintain transmission — even unvaccinated individuals protected
questions.explanation: High threshold: measles very infectious (R₀≈15). Need >95% immune to break transmission chains

questions.question: Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) like diabetes are increasing in Uganda because
options: urbanisation, changing diet, less physical activity, aging population
questions.answer: urbanisation, changing diet, less physical activity, aging population
questions.explanation: Epidemiological transition: as infectious disease controlled, NCDs increase due to lifestyle, diet, ageing

questions.question: Drug resistance in HIV treated by
options: combination antiretroviral therapy (ART) using multiple drugs targeting different steps
questions.answer: combination antiretroviral therapy (ART) using multiple drugs targeting different steps
questions.explanation: HAART (Highly Active ART): multiple drugs with different mechanisms. Resistance to all simultaneously very unlikely

questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

lessons.title: Body Defence: Skin, Inflammation and Fever

content.body:
First line of defence (non-specific):
Skin: physical barrier, keratin waterproofing, sebum (antimicrobial)
Mucus membranes: trap pathogens (respiratory, GI, urogenital)
Cilia: sweep mucus away from lungs
Natural secretions: tears (lysozyme), saliva, stomach acid (pH~2), vaginal acid

Second line (non-specific, internal):
Phagocytes: neutrophils and macrophages engulf pathogens
Inflammatory response: mast cells release histamine → vasodilation, increased permeability
Fever: high temperature inhibits pathogen reproduction, increases enzyme activity

content.title: Phagocytosis
content.body:
1. Phagocyte detects chemokines (chemical signals)
2. Moves toward pathogen (chemotaxis)
3. Engulfs pathogen in phagosome
4. Lysosome fuses → lysozymes and reactive oxygen species destroy pathogen
5. Presents antigen fragments (APCs — macrophages present to T cells)

content.title: Inflammation Signs (PRISH)
content.body:
Pain, Redness, Inflammation/Swelling, Heat
Histamine: causes vasodilation (more blood → redness/heat) and increased capillary permeability (plasma leaks → swelling)
Neutrophils recruited to site
DEBRIS cleared → healing
Chronic inflammation: linked to cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular disease

questions.question: Skin provides first line defence by
options: acting as physical barrier (keratin) and secreting antimicrobial substances (sebum)
questions.answer: acting as physical barrier (keratin) and secreting antimicrobial substances (sebum)
questions.explanation: Intact skin: toughened by keratin, waterproof, difficult to penetrate. Sebaceous glands secrete sebum (lactic acid + fatty acids = antimicrobial)

questions.question: Cilia in respiratory tract function to
options: beat to sweep mucus containing trapped pathogens away from lungs
questions.answer: beat to sweep mucus containing trapped pathogens away from lungs
questions.explanation: Mucociliary escalator: goblet cells secrete mucus (traps pathogens/particles); cilia beat in coordinated waves to move mucus to throat for swallowing

questions.question: Lysozyme in tears and saliva is an enzyme that
questions.answer: destroys bacterial cell walls
questions.explanation: Lysozyme: hydrolyses peptidoglycan in bacterial cell walls → osmotic lysis → bacteria destroyed. Natural antiseptic in secretions

questions.question: Phagocytosis destroys pathogens by
options: engulfing and digesting with lysosomal enzymes
questions.answer: engulfing and digesting with lysosomal enzymes
questions.explanation: Phagocytes (neutrophils, macrophages): engulf pathogen in phagosome, lysosomes fuse, enzymes digest pathogen

questions.question: Histamine causes in inflammatory response
options: vasodilation and increased vascular permeability → redness, heat, swelling
questions.answer: vasodilation and increased vascular permeability → redness, heat, swelling
questions.explanation: Mast cells release histamine: vasodilation → redness/heat; increased permeability → plasma leaks out → swelling (oedema)

questions.question: Fever helps fight infection by
options: creating temperature unfavourable for pathogen reproduction and activating immune cells
questions.answer: creating temperature unfavourable for pathogen reproduction and activating immune cells
questions.explanation: Fever (38-39°C): most pathogens optimally replicate at 37°C; elevated temp impairs them. Also activates phagocytes and increases metabolic rate

questions.question: Macrophages are important for adaptive immunity because
options: they present antigens on MHC to T helper cells (antigen-presenting cells APCs)
they are the same as neutrophils
questions.answer: they present antigens on MHC to T helper cells (antigen-presenting cells APCs)
questions.explanation: Macrophages: phagocytose → present digested antigen fragments on MHC II → activate T helper cells → links innate to adaptive immunity

questions.question: Natural killer (NK) cells are part of
questions.answer: non-specific innate immunity
questions.explanation: NK cells: innate lymphocytes that kill virally-infected cells and cancer cells without prior sensitisation — no antigen-specific receptor needed

questions.question: Stomach acid (pH ~2) defends against pathogens by
options: denaturing proteins and killing most ingested pathogens
questions.answer: denaturing proteins and killing most ingested pathogens
questions.explanation: Gastric HCl: pH 1.5-3 denatures proteins, destroys cell membranes of most bacteria ingested with food

questions.question: Interferon is produced by virus-infected cells and functions to
options: signal neighbouring cells to produce antiviral proteins, slowing viral spread
questions.answer: signal neighbouring cells to produce antiviral proteins, slowing viral spread
questions.explanation: Interferon: cytokine alarm signal from virus-infected cells → neighbouring cells activate antiviral gene expression → slows virus spreading through tissue

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.
